tldr: Philipsen out-sprinted a reduced bunch in Wevelgem after the late Van der Poel / Van Aert / Segaert move was caught in the final hundred metres. A pure Alpecin-Premier Tech result on the right finish for the right rider, with Tobias Lund Andresen second and Laporte third for Visma's day.

narrative.headline: Late Van der Poel / Van Aert move caught — Philipsen finishes the day
narrative.openingMoves: 240.8 km from Middelkerke to Wevelgem, multiple Kemmelberg laps with the Ossuaire turn-off. A standard Gent-Wevelgem early break that the favourites' teams kept on a manageable leash.
narrative.raceUnfolds: On the final Kemmelberg passage, a trio of Van der Poel, Van Aert, and the under-twenty-three Belgian Alec Segaert went clear. The chase took most of the run-in to assemble; the gap stayed dangerous until the final two kilometres.
narrative.decision: The bunch caught the three-man move with under 500 metres to go. Once it was a sprint, the result was Alpecin's: Philipsen has won Gent-Wevelgem before from exactly this scenario, and Van der Poel — having spent his energy on the break — was always going to leave it to him.
narrative.finale: Lund Andresen got the surprise second after a clean Decathlon lead-out gave him space; Laporte was Visma's salvage card for third, the same role he'd play two weeks later at Roubaix to keep Van Aert in position.

decisiveMoments.headline: Van der Poel / Van Aert / Segaert go clear
decisiveMoments.description: The day's defining attack came on the last Kemmelberg passage. Three riders bridged into a gap that nearly held.

decisiveMoments.headline: Bunch catches at 500 m
decisiveMoments.description: The chase organised late and absorbed the three-man move with metres to go, setting up a reduced-group sprint that Philipsen's sprint engine was always favourite to win.

teamStories.verdict: Van der Poel as decoy, Philipsen as finisher
teamStories.narrative: Alpecin's Gent-Wevelgem template — Van der Poel pulls the dangerous moves forward, the chase exhausts trying to bring it back, Philipsen wins the bunch. Worked again.

teamStories.verdict: Van Aert in the move, Laporte on the podium
teamStories.narrative: Van Aert was in the three-man late move with Van der Poel and Segaert, taking the same kind of long-shot that won him Roubaix two weeks later. Caught with metres to go; Laporte then sprinted to third from the catch, his second podium of the spring.

riderPerformances.name: Jasper Philipsen
riderPerformances.narrative: Won the bunch sprint after Van der Poel's three-man move was caught in the final 500 metres. Repeat performance on the race that suits him better than any other Belgian classic.

keyMoments.what: Won bunch sprint after late catch

riderPerformances.name: Mathieu van der Poel
riderPerformances.narrative: Joined the late three-up move with Van Aert and Segaert on the final Kemmelberg. Caught at 500 m; gave Philipsen the bunch sprint scenario that wins more often than not.

keyMoments.what: Made the late three-man move on the Kemmelberg

riderPerformances.name: Wout van Aert
riderPerformances.narrative: Joined Van der Poel and Segaert in the late move. Caught at 500 m. Visma's salvage came from Laporte's sprint for third behind.

keyMoments.what: Bridged onto Van der Poel's move on the final Kemmelberg

riderPerformances.name: Tim Merlier
riderPerformances.narrative: Held in the bunch for the late catch but couldn't match Philipsen's sprint engine. Outside the top placings.

aftermath.headline: Bunch sprint after a late catch
aftermath.body: Gent-Wevelgem went exactly as it usually goes when Van der Poel and Philipsen line up together. The leader pulls the dangerous moves; the sprinter wins the bunch when the moves are caught. Van Aert's reading of the late attack — bridging to the three-up — was identical to the Roubaix-winning move two weeks later. Same instincts, different race outcome.
